item.title: Quote of the day by Oracle co-founder Larry Ellison: "Citizens will be on their best behavior, because we’re constantly recording and reporting everything that is going on" — a dire warning on the erosion of privacy
item.description: Legendary technologist Larry Ellison has outlined a vision for an increasingly aggressive surveillance state in the 21st century
item.author: (https://www.techradar.com/author/keumars-afifi-sabet)

item.title: Gmail Thinks I'm Stupid, So I Left
item.description: Gmail's rapid descent into belittling their users, powered by AI.

item.title: A walking tour of surveillance infrastructure in Seattle
item.description: coveillance.org

item.title: Adafruit Industries - Makers, hackers, artists, designers and engineers!
item.description: electronics, open source hardware, hacking and more...

item.title: GitHub - cyberpapiii/chipotlai-max: The AI coding agent that runs on stolen Chipotle compute 🌯 Fork of OpenCode with Pepper AI as default model. Community project to add providers from Home Depot, Lowes, Target, Starbucks & more.
item.description: The AI coding agent that runs on stolen Chipotle compute 🌯 Fork of OpenCode with Pepper AI as default model. Community project to add providers from Home Depot, Lowes, Target, Starbucks & more....

item.title: Age verification for social media – the beginning of the end for a free internet? | Mullvad VPN
item.description: So-called age verification for social media is spreading across the world, framed as an effort to create a safer internet for children. In reality, age verification lays the foundation for a fully government controlled internet.

item.title: macOS Needs Its Grid Back
item.description: Two decades ago I had a better Mac desktop experience than I have today. I only had a single low res (by todays standards) screen, yet I felt like Hugh Jackman in Swordfish - deftly navigating more than nine displays without thinking, muscle and spatial memory working seamlessly together. TLDR; I built an app to return macOS spaces to its Pre-Lion Grid-enabled Glory. Read on for the increasingly rare experience of an actual human dropping a bit of nostalgia, the thinking behind why make this and some issues encountered along the way.

item.title: OpenAI frontier models and Codex are now available on AWS
item.description: OpenAI frontier models and Codex are now generally available on AWS, giving enterprises a new path to build with OpenAI through the AWS environments, controls, and procurement workflows they already use. Customers can get started with OpenAI on AWS and move faster from evaluation to production.

item.title: Tokens are the new transistors
item.description: The teams that win the next decade are the ones building as if tokens are already free and infinite

item.title: Why I dropped PRDs for ShapeUp — Luke Swithenbank
item.description: Why I dropped spec-driven development for ShapeUp, and how splitting decision clarity in two changed how we ship.

item.title: Justin Higgins — jchigg ~ dev
item.description: Software engineer, 20 years in regulated enterprise. Building at the seam between AI-assisted engineering and how that work gets recognized.

item.title: The Internet is the most stable home I have
item.description: The older I get the more I've taken note of what's stayed consistent in my life and I gotta say it ain't much. Accompanying this thought was "How have I stayed consistent for others as time moves forward?" and I gotta say I haven't.

item.title: Hardening VS Code extensions
item.description: My approach to managing VS Code extensions.
item.author: (João Palmeiro)

item.title: Message Queues and Brokers Help With Temporal Decoupling
item.description: One of the more common reactions I hear when engineers first encounter a message broker in a service-to-service communication context is that it feels like overengineering. Why introduce Kafka or RabbitMQ when a plain HTTP call does the same job with less infrastructure? It’s a fair question on the surface, and the people asking it aren’t wrong that a broker adds operational complexity. What they’re missing is that the complexity isn’t incidental — it’s the cost of a structural property that HTTP simply cannot give you: temporal decoupling.

item.title: Beyond Tokenmaxxing
item.description: Measuring leverage, not consumption

item.title: The True Expense of Nature
item.description: An exploration of Nature's endeavor (and yours) to expend as little effort as possible.

item.title: A Trip to 90s Kansai: Exploring the XD FirstClass Network BBS
Sloppy & Paste
item.description: Or "How I Learned To Keep Worrying And Just Do It Myself"

item.title: Stop Killing Games
item.description: The "Stop Killing Games" movement is making progress with the advancement of California AB 1921, a bill designed to stop developers from permanently bricking games when they shut down their servers. If you're a gamer who has watched a $70 purchase turn into a useless desktop icon overnight, you're entirely justified in your outrage. Having a software developer reach into your home and break your own software is a profound violation of trust.

item.title: Friction Was the Point — Hisham Thabet
item.description: AI may not replace us through competition, but through attrition — the slow erosion of the friction that makes us capable.

item.title: The Axis That Made the Chips
item.description: The Netherlands built its first computer in Amsterdam in 1952, moved the thinking to Eindhoven, and produced ASML. That line is history. It is also a blueprint.

item.title: Discovery vs Tokenmaxxing
item.description: Most current AI coding tools are steering users into tokenmaxxing and re-generating software from scratch, in complete ignorance of the vast amounts of publicly available, human-validated software that can be combined and modded in powerful ways to address 99% of use cases.

item.title: Growth Isn't About Doing Everything
item.description: I follow a simple flow of questions that helps me stay focused, improve consistently, and make incremental impact at workplace. I'm never running around like a headless chicken. Here are the questions I ask myself, and answer honestly, like really true to its core

item.title: Code review assumes an author
item.description: Code review assumes the person who opened the PR understands the code. Agentic development breaks that assumption. A look at why, not a fix.

item.title: Unblocked — Martín Granados García
item.description: How Claude Code unstuck a stale Swift side project, porting Next Train to the web in about an hour, and what two weeks of vibecoding taught me about AI as a creativity unblocker.

item.title: Self-Hosting on the Dark Web | David Álvarez Rosa | Personal Website
item.description: This site is now reachable over Tor as a hidden service, at a .onion address that resolves only inside the Tor network.1 Tor relays and encrypts your traffic as …
item.author: (David Álvarez Rosa)

item.title: Every byte matters
item.description: I have spent a large portion of my career working in Java. In that time, you get used to huge classes. New functionality? Just add a new method and field to the class. The cost of each new field is rarely considered. Performance is often considered from a classic computer science perspective by considering asymptotic analysis of the algorithms and data structures in-use.

item.title: I don't believe in big decisions
item.description: Big decisions are a myth. They are a long chain of small choices, and the skill is making your mistakes cheap rather than rare.

item.title: What actually runs well on a 16 GB MacBook — Prasad Khake
item.description: Honest local-LLM benchmarks on a base M3, 16 GB — tokens/sec, peak RAM, and exactly where it hits the wall. The numbers nobody publishes because they run on H100s.

item.title: More time to think
item.description: I’m finding myself thinking longer about the code I write and the features that get developed, than I did before the agentic coding era.
item.author: (https://www.facebook.com/www.ma.ttias.be/)

item.title: Why Am I Interested In Your Company? - The Collected Works of jjmojojjmojo
item.description: I've recently been looking for work, and I've been getting hung up a lot on application forms. About 3/4 of the time, there's a litany of questions sitting between

item.title: Automation is killing your startup | Tony Rice
item.description: It's not easy to prioritize human connection in a world where there's a constant war for our attention, but the ROI for authentic human connection will always win.

item.title: The Infosec Phrasebook
item.description: a/s/l/threat model?

item.title: Vector Storage Costs: S3, OpenSearch, pgvector, Pinecone
item.description: Discover the true cost of vector storage: S3 Vectors, OpenSearch, pgvector, and Pinecone. Dive into real workloads and find the best fit for your needs.

item.title: Story Points: Explicit, Honest, Predictable. Already in Use. – Daniil Bastrich
item.description: Every time a software estimate is inflated to absorb uncertainty and daily overhead, it stops measuring actual time and turns into a messy abstraction. Once your "hours" and "days" become a proxy for effort rather than the clock, you are already using Story Points implicitly. Dropping the self-deception and openly embracing this abstraction brings structure to your planning and helps reliably predict delivery without the guessing game.

item.title: Niri is not for me
item.description: After a year of using Niri, I’m probably going back to Sway - here is why. Out of sight, out of mind When you open four new terminal windows on Niri, you quite literally don’t see the consequences. If you leave those windows at their default sizes (half screen width), you have just added 4×50%=200% (two monitors’ worth) of horizontal scrolling space for your future self to sift through. You leave them there, just in case, and ‘focus left’ a bunch of times to return to what you were doing.

item.title: assignment1-basics/CLAUDE.md at main · stanford-cs336/assignment1-basics
item.description: Student version of Assignment 1 for Stanford CS336 - Language Modeling From Scratch - stanford-cs336/assignment1-basics
